instruction: Rewrite the content to meet insurance regulatory and compliance standards.

input.draft_text:
Foot Care Essentials Every Diabetic Should Follow
Your feet work really hard by carrying your entire body weight. Each one is a tiny engineering marvel comprising 26 bones, 33 joints, and more than 100 muscles, tendons, and ligaments that help you balance every day. For someone living with diabetes, these body parts need extra attention. A small cut, dry skin, or unnoticed blister can quickly turn into something serious. Read this article to know why foot care for diabetics is essential, symptoms to watch for, and foot care tips for staying healthy, active, and comfortable.
Why Foot Care Matters for People with Diabetes?
Foot problems aren’t rare in diabetes. In fact, they’re a primary reason for hospital visits. In India alone, 1 in 4 diabetes-related hospital admissions is due to foot issues. Even more worrying, diabetic foot ulcers (DFUs) carry a five-year mortality rate of 40–45%, which is higher than many cancers. Due to India’s diverse lifestyles and cultural habits, the types of bacteria causing foot infections also vary significantly, making timely care even more crucial.
Diabetes affects the feet in several ways:
Nerve damage (peripheral neuropathy): High blood sugar levels can cause numbness in your feet, making it difficult to feel heat, pain, or cold. Minor injuries often go unnoticed.
Poor blood circulation: Reduced blood flow slows healing, so even a tiny cut or blister can turn into a stubborn ulcer.
Higher infection risk: Slow healing and unnoticed wounds increase the likelihood of infections, and in severe cases, can lead to amputation.
Calluses and pressure points: Uneven pressure on the foot can lead to thickened skin, which may break down into ulcers if left untreated.
Smoking worsens it: Smoking tightens blood vessels and further blocks oxygen supply, raising the risk of serious foot complications.
Symptoms That Suggest You Should See a Doctor Soon
When you have hyperglycaemia (high blood sugar), your foot is bound to get affected, if ignored. Watch out for these signs to take early action:
Cracked skin between your toes
Unusual redness or swelling
Any wound that isn’t healing
Persistent numbness, tingling, or a sudden increase in pain
Thick, discoloured, or oddly shaped nail
Ulcers on the soles or toes
Ingrown nails
Corns, calluses, or warts
Black patches on the skin
Any discharge indicating infection
Swollen, warm foot or ankle
Charcot foot (the foot changes in shape)
Hammertoes
Tip: In any of the above situations, never try to fix them at home. Instead, seek a medical professional’s advice immediately.
Smart Daily Habits to Keep Your Feet Safe
Taking care of your feet isn’t just about comfort; it’s a vital step in preventing complications caused by ulcers, infections, and to save months of recovery.
Check your feet every day: Look for cracks, redness, swelling, colour changes, blisters, or tiny cuts. Use a mirror if needed.
Wash and dry properly: Use a mild soap and lukewarm water, pat dry, and always dry between the toes.
Moisturise wisely: Apply lotion to your heels and soles, but avoid applying it between the toes to prevent fungal infections.
Wear the proper footwear: Pick supportive shoes or custom insoles that reduce pressure and prevent friction. Avoid thin sandals, heels, and anything that cuts your feet.
Choose safe activities: Engage in gentle activities such as walking, stretching, or yoga. Skip high-impact workouts if you’ve numbness in your feet, as you may not feel new injuries.
Keep your feet warm and protected: Wear soft, diabetic-specific socks and always wear slippers at home, and comfortable shoes when outside.
Avoid harmful habits: Don’t soak feet in hot water, don’t use chemical corn removers, and avoid smoking since it worsens blood flow.
Lifestyle Practices That Lower Risks Long Term
Healthy feet depend on healthy blood flow and stable sugar levels. A few consistent choices go a long way.
Manage your diabetes well. Take your medications on time, follow your nutrition plan, and stay active for at least 30 minutes daily.
Wear properly fitted shoes. A specialist fitting reduces pressure points and hidden friction spots.
Check footwear before wearing. Remove stones, rough edges, or any shoe type that could injure your skin.
Elevate your feet daily to promote circulation and reduce swelling.
Stay alert to early warning signs. Slow-healing wounds, black patches, foul odour, persistent redness, tingling, or changes in texture should never be ignored.
Seek care early to prevent amputation due to extreme DFUs. Avoid waiting for pain, as you may not feel it because of nerve damage. Monthly check-ups with your doctor or physiotherapist are a smart habit for foot care.
Protect Your Feet, Protect Your Freedom
Staying independent begins with simple stuff, like being able to walk without pain or fear of injury. With diabetes, that freedom depends on how well you care for your feet every single day. A little attention to glucose level and feet, the proper footwear, and timely medical checks can prevent the most serious problems before they begin. Foot care is not a chore, but a daily investment in staying active, mobile, and in control of your life.
Key Takeaways
Diabetes can reduce blood flow and damage nerves in your feet, making injuries harder to notice and slower to heal.
Foot complications due to diabetes are common in India, and can become serious quickly if ignored.
Watch for warning signs, such as numbness, colour changes, wounds, swelling, warts, calluses, or thickened nails.
Daily checks, proper hygiene, quitting smoking, wearing diabetic-friendly socks, and comfortable footwear go a long way in prevention.
Stay active with low-impact movement and avoid barefoot walking to prevent accidental injuries.
Seek medical help immediately if you spot infections, blackened skin, or wounds that aren’t healing.
Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs)
How does diabetes affect the feet in the long term?
Diabetes can reduce feeling in your feet and slow blood flow, making minor injuries turn serious. Any untreated ulcers and infections can turn into gangrene and ultimately amputation.
What are the early signs of diabetic foot problems?Watch for swelling, pain, colour changes, thickened nails, blisters, cuts, pus, or a bad smell. These are warning signs of infection or tissue damage and need urgent medical attention.
What foods should diabetics avoid to protect their feet?Avoid sugary foods, refined carbs, and heavily processed meals. Choose wholesome foods: leafy greens, whole fruits, lean protein, and fibre-rich carbs to help keep blood sugar stable and reduce nerve damage risks.
How can I get treated for diabetic neuropathy in my feet?You can’t reverse neuropathy, but you can slow it down. Maintain steady blood sugar levels, stay active, maintain a healthy BMI, and take doctor-prescribed medicines. Specialists, such as physiotherapists or podiatrists, can help improve function and prevent progression.
How often should I get my feet checked by a doctor?A monthly foot exam is the best solution for individuals with diabetes. Visit a doctor immediately if you notice anything unusual in your feet. Early checks help prevent serious complications.
Disclaimer
The content of this article is intended for informational purposes only and does not provide any professional medical/nutritional advice. Kindly consult your Dietician/Nutritionist/Doctor for anyassistance/advice. Bajaj Life Insurance Limited (Formerly known as Bajaj Allianz Life Insurance Company Limited) disclaims any warranty, responsibility or liability for the accuracy of the above information and assumes no responsibility for errors or omissions.

output.final_text:
Foot Care Essentials Every Diabetic Should Follow
Your feet work really hard by carrying your entire body weight. Each one is a tiny engineering marvel comprising 26 bones, 33 joints, and more than 100 muscles, tendons, and ligaments that help you balance every day1. For someone living with diabetes, these body parts need extra attention. A small cut, dry skin, or unnoticed blister can quickly turn into something serious. Read this article to know why foot care for diabetics is essential, symptoms to watch for, and foot care tips for staying healthy, active, and comfortable.
Why Foot Care Matters for People with Diabetes?
Foot problems aren’t rare in diabetes. In fact, they’re a primary reason for hospital visits. In India alone, 1 in 4 diabetes-related hospital admissions is due to foot issues. Even more worrying, diabetic foot ulcers (DFUs) carry a five-year mortality rate of 40–45%, which is higher than many cancers.2 Due to India’s diverse lifestyles and cultural habits, the types of bacteria causing foot infections also vary significantly, making timely care even more crucial.
Diabetes affects the feet in several ways:
1. Nerve damage (peripheral neuropathy): High blood sugar levels can cause numbness in
your feet, making it difficult to feel heat, pain, or cold. Minor injuries often go unnoticed. 2. Poor blood circulation: Reduced blood flow slows healing, so even a tiny cut or blister can
turn into a stubborn ulcer. 3. Higher infection risk: Slow healing and unnoticed wounds increase the likelihood of
infections, and in severe cases, can lead to amputation. 4. Calluses and pressure points: Uneven pressure on the foot can lead to thickened skin, which
may break down into ulcers if left untreated. 5. Smoking worsens it: Smoking tightens blood vessels and further blocks oxygen supply,
raising the risk of serious foot complications.
Symptoms That Suggest You Should See a Doctor Soon
When you have hyperglycaemia (high blood sugar), your foot is bound to get affected, if ignored. Watch out for these signs to take early action:
1. Cracked skin between your toes
2. Unusual redness or swelling
3. Any wound that isn’t healing
4. Persistent numbness, tingling, or a sudden increase in pain
5. Thick, discoloured, or oddly shaped nail
6. Ulcers on the soles or toes
7. Ingrown nails
8. Corns, calluses, or warts
9. Black patches on the skin
10. Any discharge indicating infection
11. Swollen, warm foot or ankle
12. Charcot foot (the foot changes in shape)
13. Hammertoes
Tip: In any of the above situations, never try to fix them at home. Instead, seek a medical professional’s advice immediately.
Smart Daily Habits to Keep Your Feet Safe
Taking care of your feet isn’t just about comfort; it’s a vital step in preventing complications caused by ulcers, infections, and to save months of recovery.
1. Check your feet every day: Look for cracks, redness, swelling, colour changes, blisters, or
tiny cuts. Use a mirror if needed. 2. Wash and dry properly: Use a mild soap and lukewarm water, pat dry, and always dry
between the toes. 3. Moisturise wisely: Apply lotion to your heels and soles, but avoid applying it between the
toes to prevent fungal infections. 4. Wear the proper footwear: Pick supportive shoes or custom insoles that reduce pressure
and prevent friction. Avoid thin sandals, heels, and anything that cuts your feet. 5. Choose safe activities: Engage in gentle activities such as walking, stretching, or yoga. Skip
high-impact workouts if you’ve numbness in your feet, as you may not feel new injuries. 6. Keep your feet warm and protected: Wear soft, diabetic-specific socks and always wear
slippers at home, and comfortable shoes when outside. 7. Avoid harmful habits: Don’t soak feet in hot water, don’t use chemical corn removers, and
avoid smoking since it worsens blood flow.
Lifestyle Practices That Lower Risks Long Term
Healthy feet depend on healthy blood flow and stable sugar levels. A few consistent choices go a long way.
1. Manage your diabetes well. Take your medications on time, follow your nutrition plan, and
stay active for at least 30 minutes daily. 2. Wear properly fitted shoes. A specialist fitting reduces pressure points and hidden friction
spots. 3. Check footwear before wearing. Remove stones, rough edges, or any shoe type that could
injure your skin. 4. Elevate your feet daily to promote circulation and reduce swelling. 5. Stay alert to early warning signs. Slow-healing wounds, black patches, foul odour, persistent
redness, tingling, or changes in texture should never be ignored. 6. Seek care early to prevent damage due to extreme Diabetic Foot Ulcers. Avoid waiting for
pain, as you may not feel it because of nerve damage. Monthly check-ups with your doctor or physiotherapist are a smart habit for foot care.
Protect Your Feet, Protect Your Freedom
Staying independent begins with simple stuff, like being able to walk without pain or fear of injury. With diabetes, that freedom depends on how well you care for your feet every single day. A little attention to glucose level and feet, the proper footwear, and timely medical checks can prevent the most serious problems before they begin.
Key Takeaways
1. Diabetes can reduce blood flow and damage nerves in your feet, making injuries harder to
notice and slower to heal. 2. Foot complications due to diabetes are common in India, and can become serious quickly if
ignored. 3. Watch for warning signs, such as numbness, colour changes, wounds, swelling, warts,
calluses, or thickened nails. 4. Daily checks, proper hygiene, quitting smoking, wearing diabetic-friendly socks, and
comfortable footwear go a long way in prevention. 5. Stay active with low-impact movement and avoid barefoot walking to prevent accidental
injuries. 6. Seek medical help immediately if you spot infections, blackened skin, or wounds that aren’t
healing.
Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs)
1. How does diabetes affect the feet in the long term?
Diabetes can reduce feeling in your feet and slow blood flow, making minor injuries turn serious. Any untreated ulcers and infections can turn into gangrene and ultimately amputation. 2. What are the early signs of diabetic foot problems?
Watch for swelling, pain, colour changes, thickened nails, blisters, cuts, pus, or a bad smell. These are warning signs of infection or tissue damage and need urgent medical attention. 3. What foods should diabetics avoid to reduce the risk of foot complications ?
Avoid sugary foods, refined carbs, and heavily processed meals. Choose wholesome foods: leafy greens, whole fruits, lean protein, and fibre-rich carbs to help keep blood sugar stable and reduce nerve damage risks. 4. How can I get treated for diabetic neuropathy in my feet?
You can’t reverse neuropathy, but you can slow it down. Maintain steady blood sugar levels, stay active, maintain a healthy BMI, and take doctor-prescribed medicines. Specialists, such as physiotherapists or podiatrists, can help improve function and prevent progression. 5. How often should I get my feet checked by a doctor?
A monthly foot exam is the best solution for individuals with diabetes. Visit a doctor
immediately if you notice anything unusual in your feet. Early checks help prevent serious complications.
